title: Progressive Distillation
concept: Progressive distillation repeatedly halves the number of required sampling steps, training new students that match teachers using fewer and fewer steps.

exercises.prompt: If a recipe takes 100 tiny steps to cook perfectly, but you found a way to get almost the same taste in 50 steps, what would that be called?

options.text: Making the recipe taste worse on purpose
Adding more steps to the recipe

exercises.explain: Exactly! Cutting steps while keeping the result nearly the same is the whole idea behind speeding things up — which is what progressive distillation does for AI image generators.

cards.title: Why Does AI Art Take So Long?
cards.body: Some AI image generators need 1,000 tiny steps to turn fuzzy noise into a beautiful picture. That's slow! What if a 'student' AI could learn to do the same job in just a handful of steps, almost as well?

cards.title: What Is Progressive Distillation?
cards.body: Progressive distillation is a training trick where a 'student' model learns to copy a slower 'teacher' model, but using half as many steps. Then a new student learns from that student, halving the steps again — and again — until the AI can create images in very few steps.

cards.title: Passing the Baton, Faster Each Time
cards.body: Imagine a runner takes 100 small hops to cross a field. They teach a friend to do it in 50 bigger hops, reaching the same finish line. That friend teaches another to do it in 25 hops. Each new runner learns the shortcuts of the last, until someone can cross in just a few giant leaps!

cards.title: From 1000 Steps to 4
cards.body: A teacher model generates a cat picture using 1,000 denoising steps. Round 1: a student learns to match it in 500 steps. Round 2: a new student matches that in 250 steps. This keeps halving — after several rounds, a student can generate a similar cat picture in just 4 or 8 steps!

cards.title: Myth: Fewer Steps Means a Totally Different AI
cards.body: Myth: Progressive distillation builds a brand new AI from scratch with fewer steps. Truth: The student is trained to mimic the teacher's outputs directly, so it keeps very similar quality and style — it's the same 'skill,' just compressed into fewer moves.

cards.title: Teacher vs. Fast Student
cards.body: Progressive distillation halves the steps needed each round, while trying to keep the final image looking almost the same.

left.text: Needs 1,000 slow steps to make one image

right.text: Needs only 4-8 fast steps for a similar image

exercises.prompt: What does progressive distillation mainly do?

options.text: Trains new students to match teachers using fewer and fewer steps
Makes AI models bigger and slower
Deletes the teacher model completely with no training
Adds more noise to images randomly

exercises.explain: Correct! Progressive distillation repeatedly trains new student models to match a teacher's results while cutting the number of steps in half each round.

exercises.prompt: In progressive distillation, each new student model needs roughly half the steps of the previous one.
exercises.explain: True! That's the 'progressive' part — the number of required steps keeps halving round after round.

exercises.sentence: Progressive distillation trains a ____ model to match a teacher's output using fewer steps.
exercises.explain: The 'student' model learns to copy the teacher's results but with far fewer steps, making it much faster.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes progressive distillation?

options.text: It gradually reduces the number of sampling steps by training new students on previous students' outputs
It trains one giant model that never changes its step count
It only works by making images blurrier over time

exercises.explain: Right! The core idea is a chain of student models, each halving the steps needed, while staying close to the teacher's quality.

exercises.prompt: An AI teacher model needs 512 steps to draw a perfect dragon. After progressive distillation is applied 3 times (each round halves the steps), how many steps does the final student need?
exercises.explain: 512 → 256 (round 1) → 128 (round 2) → 64 (round 3). Each round halves the steps, so after 3 rounds, the student only needs 64 steps instead of 512!
